name: Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for buffer pH
principles: logarithmic relationship between pH and concentration ratio
conventions: HA is the weak acid; A^- is its conjugate base
pKa = -log10(Ka), where Ka is the acid dissociation constant at the relevant temperature
concentrations [HA] and [A^-] in any consistent units (the ratio is what matters)
valid in the buffer regime: roughly when 0.1 <= [A^-]/[HA] <= 10, i.e. pH within ~1 of pKa
assumes ideal-dilute solution; at high ionic strength replace concentrations by activities
polyprotic acids: one Henderson-Hasselbalch per dissociation step (use the relevant pKa_i)
expectedLimits: [A^-] = [HA]:  pH = pKa  (operational definition / half-neutralisation point of titration curve)
[A^-] / [HA] -> 0 (mostly protonated):  pH -> -infinity from pKa; equation breaks down — use exact equilibrium Ka = [H+][A-]/[HA]
[A^-] / [HA] -> infinity (mostly deprotonated):  pH -> +infinity from pKa; same caveat
buffer capacity beta = 2.303 [A^-][HA]/([A^-]+[HA]) — maximised at [A^-] = [HA] (pH = pKa)
outside the buffer window (|pH - pKa| > 1):  Henderson-Hasselbalch loses accuracy because the assumption that added H+/OH- doesn't significantly shift [HA] / [A^-] fails
blood plasma physiological example: H2CO3 / HCO3^- buffer, pKa1 ~ 6.1, [HCO3-]/[H2CO3] ~ 20 -> pH ~ 7.4
references: Henderson (1908) — Concerning the Relationship Between the Strength of Acids and Their Capacity to Preserve Neutrality
Hasselbalch (1916) — Die Berechnung der Wasserstoffzahl des Blutes
Po & Senozan (2001) — The Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation: Its History and Limitations, J. Chem. Educ.
